edges.description: Loss of functional alpha subunit reduces enzyme activity.
Some pathogenic HEXA alleles, especially G269S, retain residual enzyme activity and shift expression toward late-onset disease.
edges.target: Residual Hexosaminidase A activity in late-onset disease

edges.description: Diagnostic enzyme testing shows absent or reduced hexosaminidase A activity.
Reduced catabolism leads to GM2 storage in neurons.
Residual activity produces a slower phenotype dominated by cerebellar, motor neuron, and psychiatric manifestations.
edges.source: Residual Hexosaminidase A activity in late-onset disease
edges.target: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Neuronal storage corresponds to elevated GM2 ganglioside in affected brain tissue.
Neuronal lysosomal storage manifests clinically as GM2-ganglioside accumulation.
GM2 storage activates microglia and astrocytes, amplifying inflammatory neuronal injury.
GM2 storage and chronic neuroinflammation are accompanied by reduced oligodendrocyte density and forebrain demyelination.
Storage-related toxicity contributes to neuronal loss.
GM2/lipid storage also occurs in retinal ganglion cells, producing the characteristic fundus change.
Storage-related brain involvement can present with macrocephaly in infantile disease.
Inflammatory glial activation can precede and amplify neuronal loss.
Reduced oligodendrocyte density and impaired forebrain myelination produce the hypomyelination signature in infantile Tay-Sachs disease.
Retinal ganglion cell lipid storage creates the chalk-white fundus around the fovea.
Progressive neuroretinal involvement contributes to vision loss.
Progressive retinal ganglion cell lipid storage and neuroretinal degeneration produce visual impairment before or short of complete blindness.
Rapid infantile neurodegeneration causes developmental arrest and loss of attained milestones.
Early neuronal dysfunction presents with hypotonia in infantile GM2 gangliosidosis.
Brainstem and auditory pathway dysfunction produces exaggerated startle early in disease.
Progressive cortical neurodegeneration contributes to epileptic seizures.
Progressive upper motor neuron and corticospinal dysfunction contributes to late-stage spasticity.
Progressive bulbar and brainstem dysfunction contributes to swallowing impairment.
Slower childhood-onset neurodegeneration produces cerebellar, bulbar, and motor pathway manifestations.
Cerebellar involvement produces incoordination and gait disturbance.
Progressive motor involvement contributes to limb weakness and muscle wasting.
Progressive motor pathway and anterior motor neuron involvement produce generalized muscle weakness across Tay-Sachs subtypes.
Progressive motor involvement produces muscle wasting that corresponds to skeletal muscle atrophy.
Bulbar and motor speech pathway involvement produces dysarthria and speech deterioration.
Bulbar involvement contributes to swallowing impairment in juvenile disease.
Progressive motor pathway involvement contributes to increasing tone and spasm progression.
Progressive motor pathway involvement manifests as increasing spasm progression and progressive spasticity.
Chronic cerebellar involvement produces cerebellar atrophy on brain MRI in juvenile disease.
Cerebellar involvement causes gait and balance impairment.
edges.source: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Anterior motor neuron involvement contributes to progressive proximal weakness.
edges.source: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Chronic cerebellar involvement produces marked cerebellar atrophy on neuroimaging in late-onset disease.
edges.source: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Cerebellar and motor pathway involvement contributes to action tremor and stimulus-sensitive myoclonus in late-onset disease.
edges.source: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Late-onset disease can include psychotic episodes and other neuropsychiatric symptoms.
edges.source: Late-onset cerebellar and anterior motor neuron involvement

edges.description: Reduced or absent Hexosaminidase A activity is the diagnostic enzyme readout of the core Tay-Sachs enzyme deficiency.

nodes.description: Gait and balance impairment are common presenting features of juvenile and late-onset Tay-Sachs disease.
